protocol_name: Meaning in Life — General Adult Interview
description: This study explores what makes life feel meaningful to you — the people, activities, values, and experiences that give your life a sense of purpose and significance. Your responses will help researchers better understand the diverse sources of meaning that adults draw on in everyday life. We are also interested in how external conditions, including government policy, and your own personal choices might strengthen that sense of meaning. There are no right or wrong answers — every perspective is valuable, and you are welcome to share as much or as little as you feel comfortable with.

topics.guiding_question: Could you tell me which aspects of your life make it meaningful to you?
objectives: Establish rapport and invite the participant to define meaning in their own terms
Identify the initial, most salient sources of meaning the participant spontaneously raises
Surface the language and framing the participant uses to talk about a meaningful life

topics.guiding_question: Tell me about the people or communities in your life that feel most important to you — how do they figure into what makes your life feel meaningful?
objectives: Explore the nature and depth of relationships (family, friends, community) that contribute to the participant's sense of meaning
Understand how belonging, connection, or responsibility to others shapes the participant's experience of purpose
Identify specific moments or interactions that illustrate when relationships feel most meaningful

topics.guiding_question: Walk me through how your work — paid or unpaid — fits into your sense of meaning, if at all.
objectives: Understand whether and how the participant's work or productive activities contribute to their sense of purpose
Explore how contributing to something beyond oneself (e.g. society, a cause, an organisation) factors into the participant's meaning-making
Capture tensions or contradictions the participant experiences between work demands and a meaningful life

topics.guiding_question: How do your personal values or beliefs — whatever those look like for you — shape what gives your life a sense of meaning?
objectives: Identify the core values, worldviews, or belief systems (religious, philosophical, ethical) the participant draws on to make sense of their life
Explore how the participant's sense of identity connects to their experience of meaning
Understand the origins of these values and whether they have shifted over time

topics.guiding_question: Thinking about the world outside your personal life — what do you think the government or society could do to help people live more meaningful lives?
objectives: Explore which structural, social, or policy conditions the participant believes enable or undermine a sense of meaning
Identify specific government actions or societal changes the participant views as most impactful for meaning
Understand how the participant relates their personal experience of meaning to broader social or political factors

topics.guiding_question: Lastly, are there ways in which you think you could personally enhance your sense of meaning in life?
objectives: Surface the participant's sense of personal agency in actively shaping a more meaningful life
Identify concrete practices, changes, or aspirations the participant associates with deepening their sense of meaning
Explore any perceived barriers — internal or external — that the participant feels stand between them and a more meaningful life

topics.guiding_question: Is there anything about what gives your life meaning that feels important to you, that we haven't had a chance to talk about yet?
objectives: Give the participant space to raise any sources of meaning not yet captured in the interview
Invite the participant to reflect on the conversation as a whole and whether it felt representative of their experience
Close the interview respectfully and affirm the value of the participant's contribution
